Week of 9/8-9/12

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,584.29 (1.33%)
    • SPX finishes out the week hitting another all time high. Markets extended their rally this week driven by strong earnings and continued bets of interest rate cuts, with both inflation and labor data pointing to a faster pace of easing. XLK technology led gains with 3.03% followed closely by XLU utilities at 2.43%.
  • NASDAQ Composite: 22,141.10 (1.54%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 45,834.22 (0.89%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,749.88 (1.22%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Warner Bros. Discovery Inc (WBD): 18.87 (55.12%)
      • Shares of WBD led SPX gains this week following news of Paramount Skydance preparing a buy offer. While WBD plans to separate its global TV and network business, this merger could create a massive media portfolio, controlling a large portion of sports content. The stock saw its best day ever after rising more than 28% on Thursday.
    • Paramount Skydance Corp (PSKY): 87.89 (25.27%)
      • Paramount shares also rose following reports of hiring an investment bank to put together an offer for Warner Bros. CEO David Ellison appears to be trying to change the media landscape after earlier last year completing an $8 billion merger of Paramount and Skydance.
    • Oracle Corp (ORCL): 298.15 (22.13%)
      • Oracle shares skyrocketed more than 20% after reporting blockbuster growth that cloud revenue is set to grow 77% to $18 billion this year. Over the next 4 years, expectations for cloud revenue are as follows: $32 billion, $73 billion, $114 billion, and then $144 billion. Oracle and OpenAI also announced the largest computing deal in history, worth $300 billion. We've identified the customer responsible for the 359% increase in remaining performance obligations (RPO), which now total $455 billion.
    • Synopsys Inc (SNPS): 425.45 (-29.21%)
      • Shares of Synopsys fell 36% in Tuesday trading as the electronic software provider reported missing sales and lower profit outlooks. The company cited issues with its intellectual property business and negative impacts from US-China trade turmoil.

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 3,870.60 (1.55%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 26,388.16 (3.73%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225): 44,768.12 (3.03%)

European Equities

  • UK Index (UKX): 9,283.29 (0.82%)
  • German Index (DAX): 23,698.15 (-0.31%)

Commodities

  • Gold Futures: 3,680.70 (1.49%)
  • Silver Futures: 42.68 (3.50%)
    • Precious metals are hitting new all time highs amidst expectations for Fed rate cuts. As markets price in Fed easing and lower yields, some investors buy metals as a hedge against longer run inflation and uncertainty. UBS analyst predicts gold to reach 3,900 by mid next year, despite the metal rising 40% this year already. If inflation appears to be a one time impact will prices drop?
  • Crude Oil Futures: 62.11 (0.29%)

Crypto

  • Bitcoin (BTC): 115,606.90 (3.17%)
  • Ethereum (ETH): 4,634.52 (7.61%)
    • Bullish crypto? Rates coming down means “riskier” assets like crypto get bid up, and current institutional players are driving momentum by buying record inflows of BTC and ETH. Ethereum specifically, now up +142.97% in the past 6 months, has contributed to firms staking the crypto coin and trying out tokenization. I am starting to see a structural shift that may propose a sustained bull run of not just major coins, but alt coins as well.

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

Russia-India Sanctions: The US pushes its allies and G-7 countries to help impose tariffs as high as 100% on India and China for buying Russian oil. In the US pursuit to end the war in Ukraine the US has publicly ostracized Russia and any allies and have attempted economic sanctions on the countries. While the US is pushing for legal ways to take Russian assets and use them for Ukrainian defense funds, President Trump remarks his patience is waning and that banks may become a target as well. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessentt is set to meet with China in Madrid soon, while Russia currently has about $300 million in frozen funds in Europe.

Global Macroeconomic News

PPI and Core PPI: PPI comes in WAY below expectations following an awful August report. PPI comes in at 2.6% VS the 3.3% expected, while Core PPI excluding food and energy comes in at 2.8% VS the 3.5% expected. This comes in as a massive positive as a weak labor market signals for rate cuts, and Powell cutting amidst positive inflation data (inflation coming down) leads to cuts not transferring to stagflation. Service PPI saw the most noticeable drop from 2.49% YoY to 1.95%. Among this data the market rose, and we ask the question if tariffs were truly a one time impact.

Jobless Claims & Revisions: Jobless claims jumped last week to the highest in 4 years, showing more firing and less hiring. Initial claims rose 27,000 to 263,000 against the 235,000 predicted by Bloomberg for the week of September 6th. In other news, 2024 payroll revisions came in at -911K meaning that we falsely added 911K jobs last year. Showcasing two things: our country cannot correctly aggregate job data, and two, maybe Powell is way too late on rate cuts. Even with this data showing a cracking labor market, stocks continue to pump on rate cut hopes.

CPI and Core CPI: August inflation data came in slightly hotter than expected, with CPI rising 0.4% MoM to 2.9% YoY vs. the 2.9% estimate. Core CPI, excluding food and energy, rose 0.3%, but remains at 3.1% YoY. While inflation remains contained from tariffs, sticky shelter and services costs alongside goods pressures suggest price strength. Although the Fed is still expected to cut rates next week, sustained energy prices and resilient services data could complicate things as we head later into September.

IPO Season: The market saw more than $4 billion raised over six new public offerings this week. Marking the busiest period since 2021, five out of six IPOs opened above their listing price. Retail investors appear to be loving this, with many brokerages like HOOD, SOFI, and WEBULL offering pre share allocation of companies like Klarna Group, Gemini Space Station, and Figure Technology Solutions.

Europe Struggles/France Debt

Starting and focusing with France, the second largest economy in the EU behind Germany, is having a major debt crisis that is leading/coupled to political unrest and economic problems. To start, France's debt to GDP ratio is at 115% and expected to rise to 120%, with their public debt rising to €3.3 trillion this year. Last year, they spent more than they earned, creating a deficit of 5.8% of GDP, or about €160 billion. At the same time, their economy is slowing further, with a GDP growth rate suspected to drop below 1%. This means less tax collection and higher unemployment rates. What this shows is that: 1) France already has high national debt, so it has little room to borrow much more, and 2) since the deficit is very high, they must now borrow more debt to cover the gap.

So what is France doing? They are issuing bonds to fund their deficits, except the gains go toward refinancing existing debt and covering the deficit, rather than new investments or growth initiatives. Another issue with this is that by issuing so many bonds of the euro, France is indirectly influencing the credit rating of their country as well the position of the euro on the international FX market. As a result of all the new issuing, France’s sovereign bond yield is rising, meaning the country now faces higher borrowing costs (rising yield spread shown in the graph below). Fitch Ratings is scheduled to update the sovereign credit next week with many bracing for a downgrade. Compared to another EU country issuing similar bonds, this is awful for France. Tangent with this, the US and NATO are pushing for increased European defense spending while the Russia Ukraine war continues. While usually a country facing higher costs would raise taxes or cut government spending, France cannot reasonably raise taxes with their low growth rate and high unemployment. Residents in France already say that taxes/health insurance are too high and are squeezing the middle class, and reports show that many workers are leaving to find other places to live where taxes are lower and jobs are more secure. Protests towards the French government are also ramping up with yet another Prime Minister ousted, and conflicting political parties turning into a policy gridlock.

Other risks to Europe as a whole are population inefficiencies, high taxes, and low wages. Starting with a large elderly population, welfare systems take up a large portion of funding, and burdens the younger generations who seemingly cannot afford living costs amidst rising inflation. On top of this, there are also large inflows of immigration from other countries, which has little value add to the society in terms of innovative industries and skilled laborers. As a whole, euro zone countries face risk as the cost of living increases, and dropping confidence in the euro could lead to depreciation and hyperinflation. The UK economy is not necessarily doing amazing either, with the BOE cutting many times to help curb rising inflation (food, energy costs). Despite the troubles, the ECB has implemented procedures to help provide liquidity backstops and is suspected to be done cutting rates this year. Euro-area bonds fell and the euro climbed following this announcement.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones

Noah Halfon
